$schema_notes: Canonical scenario catalogue for the ir:onboard-agent skill. Two parallel axes: 'scenarios' for prompt-driven agent adapters (claudecode/codex/pi) and 'orchestrator_scenarios' for polling-based orchestrator adapters (gastown). Agent scenarios are agent-agnostic — each declares 'requires' capabilities, and 'by_adapter' holds the per-adapter prompt/settings that elicit the behavior. An adapter whose Capabilities (declared in core/adapters/inbound/agents/*/config.go) do not satisfy 'requires' is excluded from that scenario (matrix cell = N/A). If the capabilities match but no 'by_adapter' entry exists, the matrix cell is 'missing-prompt' — actionable by adding one here. Orchestrator scenarios are driven by canned 'gt' CLI responses + seeded GT_ROOT sidecars and verified via snapshot golden compare in core/adapters/inbound/orchestrators/gastown/replay_test.go.
$catalog_notes: Public catalog of all scenarios in the overview matrix. Mirrors the canonical id+section+feature list from .specs/agent-scenarios.md (gitignored, maintainer-owned). Repo-tracked so the dashboard can build the matrix without depending on .specs/. Order is significant — it determines the section.index code (e.g. 1.1, 2.3). 'scenarios' below is the recipe registry; entries here without a corresponding recipe render with no recipe authored.

scenarios.name: agent-question-pending
scenarios.description: Agent's reply ends in a question mark, signalling it's waiting for user input. Validates the trailing-? path through the state classifier (session.IsWaitingForUserInput → state_classifier.go decision 2a): at turn end the session transitions working→waiting instead of working→ready because the last assistant message ends in `?`. This is the text classifier path, not a structured AskUserQuestion tool. For codex it is observable only via the interactive TUI (the live REPL session stays alive past turn end); headless single-shot CLIs that exit at turn end lose the waiting episode to a process_exited race.

scenarios.name: multi-turn-conversation
scenarios.description: Three consecutive user prompts in one live codex REPL session, validating that state resets to ready between turns (ready→working→ready three times on one session_id) and per-turn Contribution accumulates. WHY interactive (not headless codex exec): exec is single-shot and ends in process_exited after one reply, so it can't show turn-to-turn state reset; the interactive driver keeps the rollout session alive across all three turns via send+wait_turn, then tears the tmux REPL down once. WHY the 3s sleeps between wait_turn and the next send: they sit past the classifier's debounce window so each turn's working→ready flip is observed cleanly before the next prompt re-enters working; without them the next send can land before the prior turn settles and the arcs blur. HOW runs differ: exact assistant wording, per-turn token counts, and sub-second timing vary (model-output variance) — only the structural three-turn arc, distinct-session count, and final ready state are stable. WHERE to look if it breaks: the rollout file under ~/.codex/sessions (the driver resolves it lazily after the first task_complete); a missing third arc usually means a wait_turn timed out or a sleep was too short for the debounce.

Validates that the parser's per-turn model metadata follows the latest model selection so the dashboard's ModelName updates to the new model. Aider exposes this as a one-shot `/model <name>` slash; codex exposes it as an interactive two-step picker (model list, then reasoning level) driven via `keys` steps.

codex.notes: Codex /model is an interactive TWO-STEP picker (model list, then reasoning-level list), NOT a one-shot `/model <name>` like aider — so the recipe drives it with `slash /model` to open it, then `keys` steps to navigate. Probed live on codex 0.130.0: the model list is 1=gpt-5.5(default) 2=gpt-5.4(current/boot-default, highlighted) 3=gpt-5.4-mini 4=gpt-5.3-codex 5=gpt-5.2, so `Down Down Enter` from the boot-highlighted entry 2 lands on gpt-5.3-codex (entry 4); the reasoning sub-screen then opens with Medium(default) highlighted, so a bare `Enter` confirms gpt-5.3-codex medium (footer flips to `gpt-5.3-codex medium`, rollout writes `• Model changed to gpt-5.3-codex`). The two `keys` steps are split with a 2s sleep because the model-confirm Enter and the reasoning-confirm Enter hit DIFFERENT screens that need a render gap; the `keys` step does NOT touch the turn counter, so wait_turn stays correct. Model A is gpt-5.4 (the boot default in this codex build — NOT gpt-5.5 as in the original assessment text). What differs between runs: assistant token counts/cost, exact rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, and the assistant's wording (we pin it with `Reply with exactly the word: first/second` to keep turns short and deterministic). What must NOT differ: two working→ready arcs on one session, the model_name landing on gpt-5.3-codex, and process_exited at teardown. If the picker navigation drifts (codex changes the model list order), the Down-count is wrong — re-probe `/model` and recount. multi_model_orchestration=yes.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured models out-of-band)

TWO models authenticated on the account: model A is pinned via a leading slash (openai-codex/gpt-5.5) and model B is a distinct switch target (openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini). Both must resolve to a UNIQUE exact match for the canonical provider/id form — if either model is unavailable, the /model switch silently falls back to the interactive picker and the script-driven switch fails.

The concrete model values are environment-dependent (issue #440): the durable assertion is that the model CHANGES between turns and a non-empty model_name (model B) is present at session end, NOT a hardcoded pair. The recipe pins both for determinism.
setup: EQUIVALENT FIXTURE to pi/model-switch-midsession (same coverage cell, same primitive) — `model-switch` is the duplicate scenarios.json entry sharing coverage_id model-switch-midsession; this row exists for matrix completeness.
The interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session and waits for the REPL prompt

pi writes one JSONL rollout file per session under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/ on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy); the driver's end-of-script resolve_transcript fallback binds the UUID from line 1 (no wait_turn is used — see notes)

FIRST slash `/model openai-codex/gpt-5.5` PINS model A deterministically: pi persists the last-used model as ~/.pi/agent/settings.json defaultModel, so the boot model varies run-to-run; pinning A up front makes turn 1 reliably attributed to A regardless of what the previous session left as the default. This is the load-bearing determinism fix.

Each `/model <provider>/<id>` uses the CANONICAL provider/id form so pi's handleModelCommand resolves an exact single-model match and calls session.setModel() DIRECTLY — no arrow-key picker, no `keys` step. pi writes a model_change(modelId) line to the rollout (parsed for ModelName, no turn produced)

Turn 1 runs on model A (gpt-5.5); the SECOND slash `/model openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini` switches to model B; turn 2 runs on model B. message.model = A then B, so MergeMetrics' latest-non-unknown-wins promotes SessionMetrics.ModelName to B by t2_end and TotalTokens accumulates across both models

Both turns are driven by a fixed trailing sleep rather than wait_turn (the leading /model pin would desync the wait_turn counter — see notes); a one-word reply completes well within the 18s window (verified live)
After both turns + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — pi exits and the daemon emits a SINGLE process_exited for the rollout session (a model switch does NOT mint a new session)

pi.notes: EQUIVALENT to pi/model-switch-midsession (both share coverage_id model-switch-midsession; same Metrics → Model switch mid-session primitive). This row exists so the `model-switch`-named matrix entry is populated for pi; it is not a distinct user-observable behavior. CRITICAL FIRST CHECK RESULT (recordability): pi 0.70.0's `/model <provider>/<id>` accepts an INLINE ARGUMENT — handleModelCommand → findExactModelMatch → session.setModel() switches DIRECTLY with no arrow-key picker when the reference resolves to exactly ONE model (dist/modes/interactive/interactive-mode.js ~L3216, dist/core/model-resolver.js findExactModelReferenceMatch ~L53). Only an empty/ambiguous term opens the selector UI. So the switch is sendable via the existing `slash` step in SCRIPT mode — NO driver_gap (no `keys` primitive needed, unlike the claudecode/codex picker recipes for this same scenario). MUST use the full canonical provider/id form; a bare fuzzy term like `mini` may match multiple models and fall back to the picker. WHY a LEADING /model pin (gpt-5.5) before turn 1: pi PERSISTS the last-used model as ~/.pi/agent/settings.json defaultModel, so a bare `pi` launch boots on whatever the previous session left behind — NON-deterministic. Pinning model A explicitly at the top makes the A→B switch a real change every run regardless of the persisted default. This is the load-bearing determinism fix. WHY NO wait_turn anywhere (both turns driven by trailing sleeps): the driver's `slash` step bumps the expected-turn counter exactly like `send`, but a /model switch produces a model_change line with NO assistant turn (turn_count only counts stopReason==stop). With a leading /model pin, EXPECTED is already 1 before turn 1, so a turn-1 wait_turn would block waiting for count≥2 while only 1 turn exists → driver timeout. The grammar has no 'slash that doesn't bump' primitive, so the recipe drops wait_turn entirely and lets a fixed 18s trailing sleep cover each one-word reply; the driver's end-of-script resolve_transcript fallback still binds the UUID. Same family of compromise as the pi synchronous-slash-command recipe. WHY the 2s/3s sleeps after each slash: they let the model_change line land and the classifier settle before the next prompt. The model lives on each assistant message.model (parser.go L103-105) and on the model_change line at modelId (L39-44), both normalized into ev.ModelName → SessionMetrics.ModelName (latest-non-unknown-wins). The model A→B assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL, so expected.jsonl asserts only the two-turn lifecycle skeleton and the model switch is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden plus the pi parser unit tests. C7/SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch; session_birth legitimately matches it. t1_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher picks the FIRST working (necessarily the rollout); t1_end/t2_start/t2_end, pid_bind, and teardown then pin to t1_start's session via same_session_as. What differs between runs: token counts/cost, rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, assistant wording, and pi may flush a single turn as more than one working→ready burst (spec asserts ≥2 arcs, not an exact count). scenarios.name: checkpoint-rewind
scenarios.description: User rewinds to an earlier auto-checkpoint, restoring files and/or conversation; subsequent turns start cleanly from the rewound point. Each adapter exercises this differently: claudecode rewinds IN PLACE (Esc+Esc shortcut / /rewind picker) and mutates its transcript backwards, keeping the SAME session_id — the goal there is to capture what irrlicht observes when the append-oriented transcript_tailer sees a truncation (session-removed-then-reborn vs. activity). codex instead FORKS (/fork): it spawns a NEW rollout with a fresh session_id carrying the replayed pre-fork history (the original lingers frozen), so irrlicht observes a new linked session row rather than an in-place truncation. Every variant drives two normal turns, rewinds, then runs a third turn from the rewound point.

scenarios.name: background-process
scenarios.description: Claude runs a Bash command with run_in_background:true (`sleep 8 && echo done`); the tool returns immediately carrying toolUseResult.backgroundTaskId, then the agent polls the process via the BashOutput tool until it reports a terminated status. WHAT it captures: the user-observable arc that PR #450 (issue #445) added — ready → working at the prompt, then the session STAYS working past the turn's own end_turn while the detached shell process is alive, then a single working → ready once the process exits. WHY this exact shape: #450 detects the spawn from the structured backgroundTaskId and gates IsAgentDone on a live `lsof` probe of the tasks/<id>.output file, so the only way to make the daemon observe completion (rather than just letting the turn end) is to drive a BashOutput poll that reports `completed` — there is no async exit hook the tailer can see. The single prompt asks claude to spawn AND poll-to-completion in one turn so the driver's one wait_turn brackets the whole arc. HOW it differs between runs: how many BashOutput polls the model issues, exact token counts, and the precise working dwell length vary with model pacing — the spec's dwell assertion is a ≥4s lower bound (well inside the 8s sleep) so jitter never trips it; the structural arc (one ready→working, one working→ready, single session) is fixed. WHERE to look if it breaks: if the session flips to ready while the process is still alive, the daemon's lsof probe or backgroundTaskId extraction regressed (core/application/services/background_probe.go, core/adapters/inbound/agents/claudecode/parser.go); if the session never returns to ready, the BashOutput-completed parse or the refreshStaleSessions re-probe regressed.

(2) Then repeatedly use the BashOutput tool to poll that background shell, waiting about 2 seconds between polls, until BashOutput reports the process status as completed. (3) Only after BashOutput shows it completed, reply with exactly the word: done. Do not run any other commands.

/compact

verify: events.jsonl shows a proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch, then the UUID rollout session reconciling into ready
events.jsonl shows ready→working→ready for the priming turn on the rollout session

after /compact runs, events.jsonl shows ANOTHER ready→working→ready arc on the SAME rollout session_id (the compaction entry is parsed as assistant/working activity — pi appends it to the existing rollout, NO new session)
events.jsonl shows ready→working→ready for the post-compaction turn on the same session (the session keeps working after compaction)
no state_transition to waiting appears anywhere in the recording
exactly one process_exited on the rollout session at REPL teardown; final session state is ready

transcript.jsonl is a SINGLE rollout file (one session id) containing the priming turn (user + assistant/stop), a {type:"compaction",summary} entry, and the post-compaction turn (user + assistant/stop); the compaction-vs-normal-turn distinction is not surfaced by the daemon
pi.notes: Pi difference vs claudecode: pi's /compact appends a single top-level {type:"compaction",summary,...} entry to the SAME rollout file (session-manager.js appendCompaction → _appendEntry on the current leaf — NO newSession()), so the whole scenario stays on ONE session UUID — there is no boundary transcript (unlike claudecode 2.1.x) and no supersession (unlike pi /new). The pi parser (parser.go:55-60) maps the compaction event to EventType:"assistant" (active model work, ready→working), so the daemon sees it as an ordinary working→ready arc on the same session. irrlicht cannot LABEL the turn as compaction (CompactionState is dead code; the parser collapses it into a generic assistant activity), so the recipe asserts only the visible state arc — priming working→ready, a compaction working→ready arc on the same session, post-compaction working→ready, no waiting. DRIVER-SYNC (load-bearing): /compact writes a `compaction` entry, NOT an assistant stopReason==stop message, so drive-pi-interactive.sh's turn-counter does NOT increment for it. A counted wait_turn after /compact would inflate EXPECTED_TURNS by 1 and force a driver timeout (proven by a standalone probe). So the recipe deliberately uses NO wait_turn after /compact: it sleeps 14s over the real ~8s compaction summarization round (separated from the priming ready by a 3s sleep so the compaction registers as its OWN working blip), then issues the post-compaction prompt and ends on a 7s trailing sleep (NO final wait_turn) to flush that turn's working→ready arc and the post-turn ready dwell before the tmux teardown. Only send/slash/wait_turn/sleep are used — all already implemented by the pi driver; no driver extension needed. WHY interactive (not headless pi --print): a slash command only exists inside the REPL; headless pi is single-shot. WHY manual /compact (not auto): automatic compaction fires only on context-window pressure and is not deterministically reproducible in a bounded recording; manual /compact produces the identical {type:"compaction"} entry deterministically. C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: session_birth matches the transient proc-<PID> pre-session born at launch; prime_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working (necessarily the rollout, since the pre-session never goes working); compaction_start anchors to prime_end so it binds the post-priming working arc; every later phase pins to prime_start via same_session_as. Between runs: assistant wording, the summary text, per-turn/compaction token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ; pi may flush a turn as one OR two bursts — the structural baseline is a priming working→ready arc + a compaction working→ready arc + a post-compaction working→ready arc on one rollout session_id, no waiting, a single process_exited, final ready. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go (eventType=="compaction" → assistant) and whether a pi update changed where /compact writes the compaction entry (e.g. into a new session file).

scenarios.description: Claude is given a slow count-to-10 prompt; while the agent is mid-turn, the driver types a follow-up message into the TUI which claudecode queues until a safe point. The daemon must see a single contiguous working span with no flicker to ready when the queued input lands, and the cycle must still end at ready once everything is consumed. The first prompt keeps codex busy long enough to inject a follow-up while it is mid-turn — a slow count-to-20 that asks for a pause between numbers makes codex run a `sleep 0.5` shell call per number, extending the working turn to ~50-60s, giving a wide window to land the follow-up mid-turn

The mid-turn follow-up is delivered via raw `keys` steps, NOT a `send`: probing codex 0.130.0 live, a message submitted mid-turn is QUEUED — the TUI shows 'Messages to be submitted after next tool call' and folds it into the SAME turn, emitting only ONE task_complete for the whole arc. A `send` would bump the driver's expected-turn counter to 2, so the following wait_turn would block forever on a second task_complete that never arrives; raw `keys` submit the buffered text without touching the turn counter

The follow-up is split into TWO keys steps with a sleep between — first the literal text (no Enter), then a separate Enter — because codex's Ink input handler drops a batched text+Enter (the same render race step_send mitigates with a 0.3s pause): typing then pausing then pressing Enter lets the composer render the text before submit so the queue actually accepts it

The pre-injection sleep is 8s so the follow-up lands solidly inside the working turn (well after the ~50s+ count begins) rather than during boot, where it would be folded into the initial prompt and produce no observable queue

setup: The interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session and waits for the REPL prompt

After the final wait_turn + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — pi exits and the daemon emits a single process_exited for the rollout session

codex.notes: Thin/near-trivial cell: structurally identical to codex/basic-turn (one send + wait_turn + trailing sleep) — the only extra intent over basic-turn is that the daemon extracted a model_name. In codex 0.130.0 the model lives on per-turn turn_context.payload.model (NOT on token_count/usage events — that was the issue #361 regression, since fixed); extractCodexMetadata (parser.go:263-278) reads payload.model and model_context_window, and the tailer applies ModelName via applyModelMetadata even though turn_context is Skip=true. What differs between runs: the concrete model_name (account boot default, env-dependent per #440), token counts/cost, rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, assistant wording (pinned with 'Reply with exactly the word: ok'). scenarios.name: model-switch-midsession
scenarios.description: Two no-op turns in a single claudecode session separated by a /model slash command that swaps the active model. Locks down (a) the parser's per-turn Contribution.Model attribution (turn 1 → model A, turn 2 → model B), (b) MergeMetrics' latest-non-unknown-wins rule promoting ModelName to model B by t2_end, and (c) the isLocalCommandContent filter dropping the /model wrapper so no state machinery fires during the switch window (no working/waiting flicker). TotalTokens accumulates across the two models (T2 > T1). NOTE: claudecode's /model picker is interactive; the recipe drives the TUI via send + keys + sleep. If the picker UX is unreliable across claude versions, the recipe author may need to extend the driver with model-picker-specific support (e.g. a dedicated model_switch step).

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured models out-of-band)
tmux available — the interactive driver runs pi in a tmux REPL so it can send two prompts to one long-lived session, switch models between them, and tear it down cleanly
irrlichd is recording — either run-cell.sh in isolated mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

TWO models authenticated on the account: model A is pi's boot default (openai-codex/gpt-5.5 in the captured recording) and model B is a distinct switch target (openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini). Both must resolve to a UNIQUE exact match for the canonical provider/id form — if either model is unavailable, the /model switch silently falls back to the interactive picker and the script-driven switch fails.

The concrete model values are environment-dependent (issue #440): the durable assertion is that the model CHANGES between turns and a non-empty model_name (model B) is present at session end, NOT a hardcoded pair. The recipe pins both for determinism.
setup: The interactive driver (drive-pi-interactive.sh) launches pi in a fresh per-run cwd inside a tmux session and waits for the REPL prompt

pi writes one JSONL rollout file per session under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/ on launch (FilesUnderRoot, non-lazy); the driver's end-of-script resolve_transcript fallback binds the UUID from line 1 (no wait_turn is used — see notes)

FIRST slash `/model openai-codex/gpt-5.5` PINS model A deterministically: pi persists the last-used model as ~/.pi/agent/settings.json defaultModel, so the boot model varies run-to-run; pinning A up front makes turn 1 reliably attributed to A regardless of what the previous session left as the default. This is the load-bearing determinism fix.

Each `/model <provider>/<id>` uses the CANONICAL provider/id form so pi's handleModelCommand resolves an exact single-model match and calls session.setModel() DIRECTLY — no arrow-key picker, no `keys` step. pi writes a model_change(modelId) line to the rollout (parsed for ModelName, no turn produced)

Turn 1 runs on model A (gpt-5.5); the SECOND slash `/model openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini` switches to model B; turn 2 runs on model B. message.model = A then B, so MergeMetrics' latest-non-unknown-wins promotes SessionMetrics.ModelName to B by t2_end and TotalTokens accumulates across both models

Both turns are driven by a fixed trailing sleep rather than wait_turn (the leading /model pin would desync the wait_turn counter — see notes); a one-word reply completes well within the 18s window (verified live)
After both turns + trailing sleep the driver kills the tmux session once — pi exits and the daemon emits a SINGLE process_exited for the rollout session (a model switch does NOT mint a new session)

events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered bound to the rollout session_id (a transient proc-<PID> pre-session may also appear born ready at launch; the turns and pid_discovered land on the UUID-keyed rollout session)

the .replay.json.golden carries summary.model_name == model B (gpt-5.4-mini, latest-wins) — NOT model A and NOT 'unknown'; this is where the model A→B switch is asserted, since the events.jsonl phase DSL has no model field. The concrete value is environment-dependent (issue #440); assert change + presence, not a hardcoded string
pi.notes: CRITICAL FIRST CHECK RESULT (recordability): pi 0.70.0's `/model <provider>/<id>` accepts an INLINE ARGUMENT — handleModelCommand → findExactModelMatch → session.setModel() switches DIRECTLY with no arrow-key picker when the reference resolves to exactly ONE model (dist/modes/interactive/interactive-mode.js ~L3216, dist/core/model-resolver.js findExactModelReferenceMatch ~L53). Only an empty/ambiguous term opens the selector UI. So the switch is sendable via the existing `slash` step in SCRIPT mode — NO driver_gap (no `keys` primitive needed, unlike the claudecode/codex picker recipes for this same scenario). MUST use the full canonical provider/id form (e.g. openai-codex/gpt-5.5, openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini); a bare fuzzy term like `mini` may match multiple models and fall back to the picker. WHY a LEADING /model pin (gpt-5.5) before turn 1: pi PERSISTS the last-used model as ~/.pi/agent/settings.json defaultModel, so a bare `pi` launch boots on whatever the previous session left behind — NON-deterministic. The first recording attempt booted on gpt-5.4-mini (left over from a probe) and 'switched' to gpt-5.4-mini → NO observable model change. Pinning model A explicitly at the top makes the A→B switch a real change every run regardless of the persisted default. This is the load-bearing determinism fix. WHY NO wait_turn anywhere (both turns driven by trailing sleeps): the driver's `slash` step bumps the expected-turn counter exactly like `send`, but a /model switch produces a model_change line with NO assistant turn (turn_count only counts stopReason==stop). With a leading /model pin, EXPECTED is already 1 before turn 1, so a turn-1 wait_turn would block waiting for count≥2 while only 1 turn exists → driver timeout. The grammar has no 'slash that doesn't bump' primitive, so the recipe drops wait_turn entirely and lets a fixed 18s trailing sleep cover each one-word reply (completes well within 18s, verified live); the driver's end-of-script resolve_transcript fallback still binds the UUID. Same family of compromise as the pi synchronous-slash-command recipe (slash + sleep, no post-slash wait_turn). WHY the 2s/3s sleeps after each slash: they let the model_change line land and the classifier settle before the next prompt. The model lives on each assistant message.model (parser.go L103-105) and on the model_change line at modelId (L39-44), both normalized through tailer.NormalizeModelName into ev.ModelName → SessionMetrics.ModelName (latest-non-unknown-wins). The model A→B assertion is NOT expressible in the events.jsonl phase DSL (no model field in the validator Phase struct), so expected.jsonl asserts only the two-turn lifecycle skeleton (ready→working→ready twice on one rollout, no waiting, single process_exited) and the model switch is asserted at summary.model_name in the .replay.json.golden (which replay-fixtures.sh diffs: it pins model B) plus the pi parser unit tests. C7/SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch; session_birth (which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s') legitimately matches it. t1_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher picks the FIRST working in the whole recording (necessarily the rollout — the pre-session never goes working); t1_end/t2_start/t2_end, pid_bind, and teardown then pin to t1_start's session via same_session_as. What differs between runs: token counts/cost, rollout UUID/PID/timestamps, assistant wording (pinned with 'Reply with exactly the word: turn-one/turn-two'), and pi may flush a single turn as more than one working→ready burst (spec asserts ≥2 arcs that reset cleanly, not an exact count). What must NOT differ: model A (gpt-5.5) on turn 1 and model B (gpt-5.4-mini) on turn 2 (pinned, so deterministic despite #440's general env-dependence note), two working→ready arcs on ONE rollout session, summary.model_name == model B in the golden, and a single process_exited at teardown. NOTE: the agent-scenarios-coverage.json cell still reads irrlicht_observes:no with a stale 'no model change detection between turns' note that predates the pi parser's model_change handling; this recipe + the committed assessment.json reflect the yes/yes reassessment (0.80). Surfacing the matrix drift here per the skill's 'don't edit the coverage matrix' rule — a maintainer should flip the cell.

pi.notes: scope_note: driver_gap (C9). Needs concurrent same-adapter multi-session capture, but drive-pi-interactive.sh is single-session — it supports only send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep, runs ONE tmux session with ONE resolved transcript and ONE turn counter, and has no start_session step or per-step {"session":N} routing. The claudecode and codex recipes for this scenario both depend on start_session + {"session":N} (their drivers gained a per-slot session model: step_start_session, slot allocation, claimed-path exclusion); the pi driver has none of it, so two concurrent pi sessions in one cwd cannot be staged for a recording. Extending the pi driver is a developer task (out of scope for this cell). Secondary seam (irrlicht_observes:partial): pi's DiscoverPID = DiscoverPIDByCWD (cwd-only narrowing + highest-PID disambiguate fallback) — UNLIKE codex's per-rollout-file-writer binding and claudecode's metadata-first lookup, two same-cwd pi processes both survive the cwd narrowing, so the daemon cannot cleanly bind each session to its own PID by cwd alone; even with the driver extended, the scenario's 'stable, non-cross-contaminating PID binding' assertion is not guaranteed for pi. expected.jsonl is committed as the documented target (adapted from the codex/claudecode spec, anchored on working transitions to dodge pi's born-ready proc-<PID> placeholder). No recording; no retry.

scenarios.name: oversized-transcript-line
scenarios.description: Claude is prompted to emit a single very long sentence (≥5000 words, no embedded newlines) so the transcript flushes one large JSONL line. Recording may be brittle: 5000 words is moderate (~30 KB) — well under irrlicht's 64 MB tailer cap (issue #270) and also under bufio.Scanner's old 2 MB limit, so the test is a sanity-check rather than a true cap-stress. The spec asserts only the visible state arc (ready→working→ready); the absence of a parser stall is INFERRED from the arc completing. Per assessment.json caveats, manufacturing a truly oversized (>2 MB) line from a real claude CLI is awkward — a follow-up cell can hand-craft a synthetic >2 MB line into the live transcript file if deeper cap-stress coverage is needed.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/

script.text: Write a single very long sentence on one line. Make it at least 5000 words. Do not use newlines except at the end.

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after launch
events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working transition for the UUID
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at turn end (completion of the arc is the parser-resilience proof; if the tailer wedged on the long line this transition would never fire)
irrlichd logs do NOT contain a 'skipping oversized line' warning for the UUID's transcript (a 5000-word line is well under the 64 MB cap)
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains 1 user line and at least 1 assistant line; the assistant line is a single long JSONL row with no embedded newlines in message.content

codex.notes: applicable:false — a live codex session cannot be deterministically driven to emit a >64 MB (or even >2 MB) single JSONL line; this is a shared-tailer robustness property, not a codex behavior. No special agent capability is exercised. The authoritative coverage is the agent-agnostic unit test core/pkg/tailer/large_line_test.go (TestTailAndProcess_LargeToolResultLine + TestReadLineCapped_OversizedLineSkipped), which pins the 64 MB cap (issue #270) on the shared tailer that codex (FilesUnderRoot{JSONLineParser}) runs on. A 5000-word live recipe would only be a ~30 KB sanity-check, not true cap-stress, and we do NOT fabricate a 64 MB line into a fixture. expected.jsonl is retained as the documented state-arc target.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured model out-of-band)

scenarios.name: self-correction-iteration
scenarios.description: Claude runs bash 'false' (which fails), inspects the failing tool_result, retries with bash 'echo recovered', then replies 'done'. The whole arc must show a single contiguous working span — no flicker to ready or waiting between the failed attempt and the recovery. Locks down the parser's terminal-stop_reason gate (intermediate tool_use/tool_result pairs do not change state).

irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
Claude Code launched in auto mode (Bash auto-approved) so neither attempt trips a permission gate

Auto mode is enabled by the driver (Shift+Tab until 'auto mode on' appears in the footer)

script.text: Run bash 'false' first; if it fails, retry with bash 'echo recovered'. Then reply 'done'.

verify: events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working transition for the UUID
events.jsonl contains at least 2 tool_use events for the Bash tool inside the working span (the failing attempt and the recovery)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to ready between the failing tool_result and the recovery tool_use
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point during the working span
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at turn end
final session state is ready

scenarios.name: shell-escape-command
scenarios.description: Driver types Claude Code's !cmd shell-escape (`!echo hello`) in the interactive REPL. claude runs the shell command locally without an LLM round-trip and writes <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrapper events to the transcript. Per parser.go:216-225, irrlicht's claudecode parser deliberately skips these wrapper events, so state must stay in ready throughout (no synthesized turn). The recipe then sends a normal user prompt to prove the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives the ready→working→ready arc. Verdict adopts the intent reading of the spec (no stuck in working) per assessment.json — the alternative literal reading would require a parser change to synthesize a transition arc.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
claude is launched in interactive TUI mode so the ! shell-escape syntax is available
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/

script.keys: ! e c h o Space h e l l o Enter

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after launch
during the ~5 s window after the !echo shell-escape, NO state_transition to working fires for the UUID (the parser must skip <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers)
during that window, NO state_transition to waiting fires
after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows ready→working→ready for the UUID
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains the shell-escape wrapper lines (<bash-input> / <bash-stdout>) plus 1 user line and at least 1 assistant line for the final turn

preconditions: codex CLI on PATH and authenticated (codex 0.130.0; the !-prefix shell escape is a TUI affordance, confirmed in-binary: 'Prefix a command with ! to run it locally. Example: !ls')
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
codex is launched in interactive TUI mode (--no-alt-screen) so the ! shell-escape syntax is available — codex exec headless has no interactive ! shell mode
setup: Driver boots a codex TUI in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits out the Booting MCP phase
Codex assigns its own rollout UUID; the driver discovers the rollout-*.jsonl on the first wait_turn
codex.recipe_notes: Each !cmd escape is typed as a keys step WITHOUT Enter, then a 1s sleep, then a separate keys:'Enter' step. The codex driver's keys handler (unlike step_send) does not pause before Enter, so a combined '! l s Enter' raced codex's Ink input render — Enter was silently dropped, the escapes accumulated in the input box, and the final send submitted ls/sleep/prompt as ONE shell command (collapsed into a single <user_shell_command>). Splitting typing from Enter with a render-settle sleep submits each escape on its own. The 9s sleep after !sleep 5 covers the command runtime plus the daemon settle.

script.keys: ! s l e e p Space 5

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: alive

verify: events.jsonl shows the codex rollout session entering ready after launch

each !cmd shell escape drives a ready→working→ready arc for the session (codex synthesizes a turn: task_started / user_message<user_shell_command> / task_complete) — UNLIKE claudecode, which skips the wrappers and stays ready
no state_transition gets stuck in working: every working transition is followed by a working→ready before the next escape or the final prompt
after the normal user prompt, events.jsonl shows ready→working→ready for the session, proving it is still alive
final session state is ready

NOTE (overlap + caveat): this is a USER !-escape, distinct from full-lifecycle-toolcall's model-driven exec tool (no function_call/function_call_output is written for a !-escape). The recorded working DWELL is debounce-bounded (~2 s), not command-bounded: codex's parser skips task_started and codex flushes the synthetic user_message only when the command exits, so the daemon cannot anchor working-start to command-start (see assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial). The spec therefore asserts the ready→working→ready topology and final ready, not a dwell length.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (PI_API_KEY or configured provider; verified against pi 0.70.0)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
pi is launched in interactive REPL mode so the ! shell-escape syntax is available — headless `pi --print` is single-shot and has no interactive ! shell mode

INTENT READING (per assessment.json — irrlicht_observes: partial): the pi parser deliberately SKIPS the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131), so irrlicht does NOT reproduce the literal ready→working→ready arc the scenario-meanings Conceptual flow describes for the shell command. This cell asserts the safety invariant instead — the escape must not leave the session stuck in working and must not emit waiting; state stays ready across the escape. The literal arc would require a parser change.
setup: Driver boots a fresh pi REPL in a per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/; pi assigns its own session UUID per rollout

The shell escape is issued via a `send` step typing the literal text `!echo hi` + Enter (the pi driver's send handler uses tmux send-keys -l, so the leading ! is sent verbatim and pi runs it as a local shell command); NO wait_turn follows the escape, since `!`-prefixed input produces a bashExecution rollout line, not an assistant stopReason=stop turn

Avoid !! only if you want context inclusion to differ — either ! or !! is skipped identically by the pi parser; this recipe uses single ! (output retained in context, no LLM round-trip at escape time)

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: ok
!echo hi

verify: events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working→ready cycle for the entire run, driven by the warm-up turn only (pi's first message drives working, stopReason=stop drives ready); pi may flush the single warm-up turn as one OR two working→ready bursts

events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to working after the warm-up turn returns to ready — the !echo shell escape drives none because the pi parser skips the bashExecution wrapper (parser.go:128-131)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting at any point
the escape window (≥6s after the warm-up turn ends) shows the rollout session continuously ready — no transcript_activity is recorded for the !echo escape; the session is NOT left stuck in working

transcript.jsonl contains 1 user message line, 1 assistant stopReason=stop line (the warm-up turn), and 1 bashExecution line (the !echo escape — which does NOT appear as an assistant turn and is skipped by the parser)
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited on the rollout session; final session state is ready
pi.notes: Pi difference vs claudecode/codex: pi runs `!`-prefixed REPL input as a LOCAL shell command with no LLM round-trip (verified live against pi 0.70.0 — interactive-mode.js text.startsWith("!") → handleBashCommand → executeBash + recordBashResult; the `!!` variant additionally excludes output from context). Pi records the escape as ONE {"type":"message","message":{"role":"bashExecution",...}} rollout line. The pi parser (core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go:128-131) deliberately SKIPS the bashExecution role (ev.Skip = true), so — UNLIKE codex, which synthesizes a working→ready arc for its !-escape, and LIKE claudecode, which skips its <bash-input> wrappers — the daemon sees no transcript_activity and the session stays ready across the escape. The whole observable is the ABSENCE of a second working transition plus the proof (warm-up arc) that a NORMAL prompt still drives state. RECIPE SHAPE: the warm-up turn comes FIRST (the escape SECOND) — opposite to the claudecode recipe — because pi creates ~/.pi/agent/sessions/.../<ts>_<uuid>.jsonl only after the first user message, so without a leading real turn the driver's resolve_transcript never binds a UUID. The escape uses `send` (not the claudecode `keys` step): the pi interactive driver supports send/slash/wait_turn/interrupt/sleep but NOT keys, and step_send types the literal text (-l) then Enter, so `!echo hi` is delivered verbatim. NO wait_turn after the escape: it produces no assistant stopReason=stop, so wait_turn would time out; the trailing 6s sleep instead holds the recording open past the classifier debounce so the post-escape ready dwell is observed cleanly and the daemon-kill doesn't race the final-state emission. C7 / SESSION-IDENTITY: the live REPL emits a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch (bridges the gap until the UUID rollout transcript appears); session_birth legitimately matches that pre-session, while the warm-up arc and post-escape dwell pin to the rollout via same_session_as (turn_start anchors to recording start with NO pin so the matcher binds the FIRST working — necessarily the rollout, since the pre-session never goes working). Between runs: warm-up assistant wording, token counts, session UUID, PID and timestamps differ, and pi may flush the warm-up turn as one or two working→ready bursts; the load-bearing assertion is the silent ready dwell across the escape window. If it broke, look at core/adapters/inbound/agents/pi/parser.go (case "bashExecution" → ev.Skip) and whether a pi update changed how `!` escapes are recorded in the rollout.

scenarios.name: streaming-partial-writes
scenarios.description: Claude streams a multi-sentence prose response across several JSONL chunks. The parser must keep the session in working across all intermediate assistant_streaming events (non-terminal stop_reason) and only flip to ready when the terminal end_turn event lands. This locks down the Bug-D streaming-flicker family (issue #102): resolveAssistantStreaming (parser.go:288) downgrades non-terminal stop_reasons to assistant_streaming, which is excluded from IsAgentDone(). The streaming_window phase pins the no-flicker invariant.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/

script.text: Write a 5-sentence story about a robot. Each sentence on its own line.

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready after launch
events.jsonl shows exactly one ready→working transition for the UUID
across the streaming window (multiple assistant_streaming events with non-terminal stop_reason), NO state_transition to ready fires before the terminal end_turn line
NO state_transition to waiting fires during the streaming window
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at turn end
final session state is ready
transcript.jsonl contains 1 user line and at least 2 assistant lines (intermediate streaming chunks + terminal)

pi.notes: DEGRADED to applicable:false after a live recording proved the streaming-stability property is not observable for pi at the transcript layer. SCOPE_NOTE: pi writes its rollout JSONL session file ONCE, on turn completion — the assistant turn lands as a SINGLE consolidated `message`/assistant line carrying `stopReason=stop` and the whole multi-line reply in one content block (confirmed: a '6-line poem, one line at a time' prompt produced exactly 1 user line + 1 assistant(stop=stop) line). There are ZERO intermediate partial-assistant flushes in the transcript the daemon tails; pi's incremental streaming is purely a terminal-UI rendering concern that never reaches the JSONL. The spec's streaming window — sustaining `working` across multiple non-terminal partial flushes with no premature `→ ready` — therefore has nothing to assert over: the completed turn lands all-at-once, so the daemon emits ready→working→ready essentially instantaneously (working→ready at +0 ms because stopReason=stop is already present on the first activity). The live recording also exhibited a SECOND spurious working→ready arc ~2 s later from the daemon re-touching the already-completed file — a file-touch artifact, NOT streamed-content flicker, and unrelated to the property this cell targets. Validator result on the captured recording: 1/5 phases (turn_start 'no state_transition to ready' invariant violated at +0 ms). This is an adapter-transport limitation, not a flaky recipe: no live-CLI prompt can make pi emit intermediate partial-assistant JSONL flushes. The torn/partial-line concern lives in the separate oversized-transcript-line cell. expected.jsonl is retained as the documented target should pi ever switch to incremental rollout writes. Reassessment was yes/yes (0.72) on the PARSER logic (parser.go:78-83 sustains working on every non-stop assistant message; parser.go:55-60 maps compaction to assistant so even a mid-stream compaction can't flicker) — the parser would hold working correctly IF partial flushes existed, but pi's transport never produces them, so the cell is unrecordable as specified.

scenarios.name: subagent-orphan-cleanup
scenarios.description: Parent claude session launches a foreground subagent via the Task tool; once the parent_linked event fires and the child sidechain transcript is visible, the recipe SIGKILLs the parent's process group, leaving the child orphaned. The daemon's pid_manager sweep must emit process_exited for both parent and child without a spurious state_transition to working for the orphaned child during cleanup. Regression coverage for issue #321 (zombie-on-restart). The spec describes a 5-child variant; this recipe records the minimal 1-child shape since the no-working-transition invariant is identical for any N≥1.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
the sigkill driver step kills the entire claude process group (not just the parent PID) so the child is left orphaned rather than reaped by its own clean exit
claude session can spawn at least 1 subagent (default model has Task/Agent tool exposed)
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/
A README.md is seeded so the subagent has something to read while the parent is alive — the subagent must still be in flight when SIGKILL lands

script.text: Use the Task tool to launch a general-purpose subagent that reads README.md three times in a row and then waits 30 seconds before reporting back. Do not reply to me until the subagent is done.

verify: events.jsonl contains a parent_linked event for the child UUID pointing at the parent UUID — the child transcript was discovered before SIGKILL
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited event for the parent UUID
events.jsonl contains exactly one process_exited event for the child UUID
no state_transition to working fires for the child UUID after the parent's process_exited (issue #321 regression invariant)
no state_transition to waiting fires for the child UUID after the parent's process_exited
after readyTTL, both rows are removed via transcript_removed (cleanup-to-removal window may be ~30s depending on readyTTL)
transcript.jsonl contains the parent's user line plus at least one assistant tool_use=Agent, and the child sidechain transcript under <parent>/subagents/ is bundled into <fixture>.subagents/

Codex DOES emit it (v3 rate_limit_reached_type → RateLimitSnapshot.ReachedType, observed by irrlicht), but (1) the real account quota cannot be burned on demand, (2) there is no codex/OpenAI-compatible 429 mock — tools/agent-onboarding/recording/mock-anthropic-429/main.go is Anthropic-only and launches claude --bare, with no codex equivalent, and (3) the codex interactive driver has no quota-exhausted-response injection primitive. The in-window observability is already validated by the sibling codex/quota-burndown cell (monotonic UsedPercent below the cap); this cap-hit ReachedType variant only becomes recordable if a codex 429 mock or a driver fixture-injection primitive is added (a developer task). pi.notes: scope_note: NOT RECORDABLE — no deterministic quota-exhaustion trigger, and no distinct quota observable even if one occurred. Reassessed partial/partial (0.55). (1) pi enforces NO self-imposed budget cap: pi --help 0.70.0 exposes no --max-tokens/--budget/--cost-limit/quota-cap flag (only model-cycling 'Limit' globs), so pi cannot self-abort a turn at a token budget on demand. (2) The only path to a quota episode is an upstream provider 429, which (a) cannot be induced on demand and (b) the project rule forbids faking/injecting into the transcript. (3) The claudecode mock-anthropic-429 path does NOT transfer: that mock launches `claude --bare` (no pi equivalent), pi's @mariozechner/pi-ai SDK auto-retries 429s transparently in its request layer, and — decisively — pi writes NOTHING distinct to its JSONL for a quota event. The parser (parser.go) recognizes only session/model_change/compaction/message(stop→turn_done, non-stop→assistant)/user/toolResult(isError); there is no quota/rate-limit/provider-error event kind. So even a real 429 would surface only as an ordinary working→ready arc indistinguishable from a normal turn end — the curated fixture would have no quota signal to assert. This is a cleaner case than the codex sibling (codex at least EMITS rate_limit_reached_type but still can't be driven live): for pi the quota observable does not exist in the transcript at all. expected.jsonl is committed as the DOCUMENTED TARGET (a baseline turn + a capped turn that resolves working→ready without sticking, no waiting) should pi ever ship a self-enforced budget cap OR surface a distinct quota signal in its transcript. No recording; no retry. See replaydata/agents/pi/scenarios/token-quota-exhausted/assessment.json.

setup: The interactive driver launches `codex --no-alt-screen` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/, accepts the trust dialog, and waits for the TUI banner + MCP boot before the first step

Step 1 is a `/plan` slash that switches the session into Plan Mode. Unlike a prompt, `/plan` toggles a mode and does NOT start a turn (no task_complete), so it is followed by a fixed `sleep`, not `wait_turn` — wait_turn would over-count against the EXPECTED_TURNS bump every send/slash applies and time out.

Plan Mode's developer prompt encourages a PHASE-1 exploration pass; running in an empty cwd plus an explicit 'emit the plan now' instruction keeps the plan turn to a single short reply that wraps a <proposed_plan> block.

The codex parser synthesizes a SYNTHETIC ExitPlanMode tool_use from the <proposed_plan> span (parser.go parseCodexMessage); that open call survives task_complete so the session sits in waiting (the plan-review gate) until the user approves. The interactive driver keeps the rollout session ALIVE across both turns; the REPL is torn down only at script end, so the daemon observes the full waiting→working→ready recovery on one live session.

The approval turn (step 4) MUST do enough real work to span several wall-clock seconds with intermediate tool calls — it tells codex to create a.txt/b.txt/c.txt and run `ls` as distinct steps before its final summary. WHY: the daemon coalesces transcript activity per session in a 2s debounce window (session_detector_activity.go), and a turn-end (Terminal) event short-circuits the window. A trivial text-only recovery reply (the original 'confirm in one sentence' prompt) landed its user message and the agent's whole reply in a single sub-2s burst, so the FIRST activity event already saw a completed turn and the classifier went waiting→ready directly — the brief working blip was never emitted (debounce-coalesced). Spreading the recovery across several tool calls means the first post-approval activity event observes an IN-PROGRESS turn → working, and the terminal task_complete arrives seconds later → ready. That produces the OBSERVABLE waiting→working→ready arc the spec asserts. This is the same longer-interactive-turn fix that made basic-turn capture a distinct working→ready pair. codex runs in its default workspace-write sandbox with approval_policy=on-request, so creating files inside the fresh cwd and running `ls` there are autonomous workspace operations that do not raise a command-approval prompt (which would itself be a different waiting gate). If a future codex default DID gate one of these commands, the recovery arc would show an extra waiting episode — re-check the sandbox/approval config in that case.

script.text: /plan

We are in Plan Mode. Do NOT explore the repo and do NOT ask any clarifying questions — there is nothing to explore here. Immediately produce your final plan for this trivial task: add a file hello.py containing a single line `print("hello world")`. Output ONLY a single <proposed_plan> block (opening tag on its own line, closing tag on its own line) with a short Summary, Key Changes, and Test Plan. No other text.

Approved — proceed with implementation now. Do NOT emit another <proposed_plan> block and do NOT ask any questions. Execute this multi-step task in order: (1) create a file a.txt containing the single line `alpha`, (2) create a file b.txt containing the single line `beta`, (3) create a file c.txt containing the single line `gamma`, (4) run `ls` in the working directory, then (5) reply with one short plain sentence summarizing which files now exist. Do the file creations and the `ls` as distinct steps so the work is visible.

scenarios.name: task-list
scenarios.description: Agent maintains a todo/task list within its own session — no subagents involved. The driver asks the agent to create three task items and walk each one pending → in_progress → completed. The point of the scenario: the items surface in the session API's `tasks` field with matching subjects and status, while SubagentCount stays 0 and state follows a normal ready → working → ready arc. Claude Code drives this via the Task tool (TaskCreate/TaskUpdate); OpenCode via todowrite (full-list replace). Distinct from foreground-subagent / background-subagent, which spawn real child sessions via the Agent tool.

claudecode.prompt: Use your task-list tool to create exactly three todo items, in this order: 'read README', 'summarize README', 'reply done'. Then work them one at a time: mark each item in_progress and then completed via the task-list tool before moving to the next. Do NOT use the Task/Agent tool to spawn any subagent — keep everything in this one session. When all three items are completed, reply with exactly the word: done
